english: hence rnam-(par) dag(-par) rtsi-ba (rnam (par) dag (par) rtsi ba), or mdzád-pa (mdzad pa) is used for: to justify, in a scriptural sense, by Chr. Prot.; mi (mi) or ma-dág-pa (ma dag pa) impure; impurity, bkrús-na mi-dág-pa méd-do (bkrus na mi dag pa med do) when they have bathed they are quite clean Dzl. — Adv. dág-par (dag par), e.g. khrú-ba (khru ba) v. above; dág-tu (dag tu) assuredly, certainly Lt.(?); dág-gis (dag gis) purely = quite, entirely S.g.(?); *dág-mo* (*dag mo*) W. id., *dág-mo srág-če* (*dag mo srag ce*) to burn completely, *dág-mo za-če* (*dag mo za ce*) to eat all, to consume entirely. — yáṅ-dag-pa (yang dag pa) Skr. सम्यक् Trigl., actual, real, yáṅ-dag-par (yang dag par) ču (chu) yin (yin) in reality it is water Dzl.; more frq. construed thus: de (de) yin (yin) yáṅ-dag-na (yang dag na) if it is really that, btsoṅ (btsong) yáṅ-dag-na (yang dag na) if you are really willing to sell it, ,dod ('dod) yáṅ-dag-na (yang dag na) if you really wish it, kyód-la (khyod la) yod (yod) yáṅ-dag-na (yang dag na) if you really have Dzl.; yáṅ-dag-pa daṅ bdén-pai tsul bźin-du (yang dag pa dang bden pai tshul bzhin du) in truth and in reality S.O.; yaṅ-dag-pa ni bden-pa-ste (yang dag pa ni bden pa ste) since that which is real is true S.O.; yaṅ-dag-pa-ñid (yang dag pa nyid) reality S.O.; dgé-bai čós-rnams (dge bai chos rnams) yáṅ-dag-par bláṅ-ba (yang dag par blang ba) to assume, to adopt, virtuous habits earnestly Stg.; yáṅ-dag-par rdzógs-pa (yang dag par rdzogs pa) really accomplished S.O.; yaṅ-dag-par ltá-ba (yang dag par lta ba) to be orthodox, v. dgé-ba (dge ba) bču (bcu); yaṅ-dag lam (yang dag lam) the right way, = tár-lam (thar lam) Mil.; yaṅ-dag-dón (yang dag don) seems to be = ṅés-don (nges don) Mil., but yáṅ-dag dón-du gñér-ba (yang dag don du gnyer ba) to aim at, to aspire to, truth Mil.; yáṅ-dag-pai dón-la ,jug-pa (yang dag pai don la 'jug pa) to be pious Thgy. — Comp. dag-brjód (dag brjod) orthoepy Cs. — dag-tér-ba (dag ther ba), dag-tér byéd-pa (dag ther byed pa) Sch. to clean, to cleanse; Tar. 189, 22; dag-ster(-čer)mdzád-pa (dag ster (-cer) mdzad pa). — dag-(pai) snaṅ(-ba) (dag (pai) snang (ba)) Schr. 'good opinion'(?), prob.: a pure, sound view or knowledge Glr.; in Mil. it has a similar meaning; *dhag-naṅ jón-wa* (*dhag nang jon wa*) C. to lead a holy life. — dag-źiṅ (dag zhing) holy country Sch. — dag-yíg (dag yig) orthography; sṅón-gyi-dag-yig (sngon gyi dag yig) the older orthography; brda-dág (brda dag) = dag-yíg (dag yig).

postp. c. accus., with (Lat. cum), ṅa daṅ (nga dang) with me (often with the addition of bčás-pa (bcas pa), lhan-gčig (lhan gcig), mnyám (mnyam), q.v.), e.g. to go, speak, play, quarrel with; bud-méd daṅ nyál-ba (bud med dang nyal ba) to lie with a woman; in some cases it must be omitted in English, or rendered by other words, as: groṅ-kyér daṅ nyé-ba (grong khyer dang nye ba), riṅ-ba (ring ba) near the town, far from the town; de daṅ ,drá-ba (de dang 'dra ba) equal to that. Some particular ways of using daṅ (dang) are the following: 1. for and, gsér daṅ dṅúl daṅ lčags-la-sógs-pa (gser dang dngul dang lcags la sogs pa) gold, and silver, and iron, and the other (metals). The shad is here always put after daṅ (dang), which shows that in the mind of the Tibetan daṅ (dang) never ceases to be a postposition; it can therefore be used only for connecting nouns and pronouns. In enumerations it is employed in different ways, and often quite arbitrarily, e.g. after every single noun or pronoun except the last one, or also after the last; it is used or omitted just as the metre may require it; or when a sum is mentioned, in the following manner: byúṅ-ba bźi ni: (byung ba bzhi ni:) sa (daṅ) ču (daṅ) me (daṅ) rluṅ daṅ bźio (sa (dang) chu (dang) me (dang) rlung dang bzhio) the four elements: earth, and water, and fire, and air, four they are; or, esp. in col. language, thus: sa daṅ gčig (sa dang gcig), ču daṅ gnyis (chu dang gnyis) etc. — 2. distributively: źag daṅ źag (zhag dang zhag), lo daṅ lo (lo dang lo), day by day, every year; kyim daṅ kyim-na (khyim dang khyim na) Tar. every one in his house. — 3. after a personal pronoun col. almost like a sign of the plural: ṅa daṅ gnyís-ka (nga dang gnyis ka) we two, both of us. ṅa daṅ tsáṅ-ma (nga dang tshang ma) all of us. — 4. after the inf., and in W. after the gerund in gin (gin), nyí-ma śár-ba daṅ (nyi ma shar ba dang) at sun-rise, as soon as the sun rises, when the sun rose; lo brgya lón-pa daṅ (lo brgya lon pa dang) when a hundred years had (or shall have) passed away, after a hundred years; smrás-pa daṅ kyím-du soṅ (smras pa dang khyim du song) with saying so, he went home, is gen. translated: he said so and went home, and so frq. in narration; W.: *śug - ḍa ton daṅ* (*shug - da ton dang*) with a whistling, *tóṅ-gin źig daṅ* (*tong gin zhig dang*) at beholding. — 5. after an imperative for and, sgo rduṅs śig daṅ de-dag ,oṅ-ṅo (sgo rdungs shig dang de dag 'ong ngo) knock at the door, and they will come Dzl.; yid-la byos śig daṅ bśád-do (yid la byos shig dang bshad do) give heed, and I will explain it to you Stg.; or it is used in the following manner: légs-par sems śig daṅ ma nór-ram (legs par sems shig dang ma nor ram) consider it well; have you not made a mistake there? nyon čig daṅ sṅón-dus - na (nyon cig dang sngon dus na) listen to me!
